1.o: Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, in the first year of his reign, took Jerusalem, and carried whom and what he pleased away. From this first captivity, most think the seventy years are to be dated. It is the interest of princes to employ wise men; and it is their wisdom to find out and train up such. Nebuchadnezzar ordered that these chosen youths should be taught. All their Hebrew names had something of God in them; but to make them forget the God of their fathers, the Guide of their youth, the heathen gave them names that savoured of idolatry. It is painful to reflect how often public education tends to corrupt the principles and morals.
1.m:
Nebuchadnezzar, the king of Babylon, captured Jerusalem in the first year of his rule and took whoever and whatever he wanted. Most people believe that the seventy years of exile started from this event. 

It’s good for leaders to use smart people, and it’s smart for leaders to find and teach those who will help them. Nebuchadnezzar told people to choose young boys and teach them. The boys’ Hebrew names all included God’s name. But to make them forget about the God their parents worshipped—their guide—people gave them new names that honored false gods instead. 

It’s sad to see how often schools end up teaching kids bad values and beliefs.

1.o: The interest we think we make for ourselves, we must acknowledge to be God's gift. Daniel was still firm to his religion. Whatever they called him, he still held fast the spirit of an Israelite. These youths scrupled concerning the meat, lest it should be sinful. When God's people are in Babylon they need take special care that they partake not of her sins. It is much to the praise of young people, not to covet or seek the delights of sense. Those who would excel in wisdom and piety, must learn betimes to keep the body under. Daniel avoided defiling himself with sin; and we should more fear that than any outward trouble. It is easier to keep temptation at a distance, than to resist it when near. And we cannot better improve our interest in any with whom we have found favour, than to use it to keep us from sin. People will not believe the benefit of avoiding excess, and of a spare diet, nor how much they contribute to the health of the body, unless they try. Conscientious temperance will always do more, even for the comfort of this life, than sinful indulgence.
1.m: We must admit that anything good we achieve comes from God. Daniel stayed true to his faith. He kept the spirit of being an Israelite no matter what others said. The young men were careful about the food, worried it might be against their beliefs. When God’s people live among those who don’t follow Him, they need to avoid doing wrong things. Young people earn respect by not wanting worldly pleasures. To become wise and godly, one must control their body early on. Daniel refused to eat food that could harm him spiritually. We should fear sinning more than facing any other hardship. It’s easier to stay away from temptation than to fight it once you’re close. Using our influence wisely means asking for help to avoid sin. People often doubt the benefits of eating simply and staying healthy until they try it themselves. Being careful about what we eat and drink actually makes life better than giving in to unhealthy desires.

1.o: Daniel and his fellows kept to their religion; and God rewarded them with eminence in learning. Pious young persons should endeavour to do better than their fellows in useful things; not for the praise of man, but for the honour of the gospel, and that they may be qualified for usefulness. And it is well for a country, and for the honour of a prince, when he is able to judge who are best fitted to serve him, and prefers them on that account. Let young men steadily attend to this chapter; and let all remember that God will honour those who honour him, but those who despise him shall be lightly esteemed.
1.m:
Daniel and his friends stayed true to their faith. Because of this, God gave them great knowledge and wisdom. Young people who follow God should try harder than others at school and work. They shouldn’t do it for people’s approval, but to show how good the message of Jesus is. This way, they can help others too. It’s good for a nation and its leader when they can find the best people to help run things. 

Young men should pay attention to what Daniel did. Everyone should know that God honors those who honor Him, but He won’t think much of those who don’t respect Him.

2.o: The greatest men are most open to cares and troubles of mind, which disturb their repose in the night, while the sleep of the labouring man is sweet and sound. We know not the uneasiness of many who live in great pomp, and, as others vainly think, in pleasure also. The king said that his learned men must tell him the dream itself, or they should all be put to death as deceivers. Men are more eager to ask as to future events, than to learn the way of salvation or the path of duty; yet foreknowledge of future events increases anxiety and trouble. Those who deceived, by pretending to do what they could not do, were sentenced to death, for not being able to do what they did not pretend to.
2.m:
The most important people often have the biggest worries and problems that keep them awake at night. Meanwhile, hardworking people get good, peaceful sleep. We don't know how uncomfortable some rich and fancy people really are, even if others think they're living happily.

The king told his wise men that they had to tell him what he dreamed, or else they would all die as liars. People are much quicker to want to know about things that will happen in the future than to learn about God’s ways or how to behave properly. Knowing about future events just causes more worry and stress.

Those who lied by saying they could do something they couldn’t actually do were sentenced to death because they failed to do what they claimed they could do.

2.o: Daniel humbly prayed that God would discover to him the king's dream, and the meaning of it. Praying friends are valuable friends; and it well becomes the greatest and best men to desire the prayers of others. Let us show that we value our friends, and their prayers. They were particular in prayer. And whatever we pray for, we can expect nothing but as the gift of God's mercies. God gives us leave in prayer to tell our wants and burdens. Their plea with God was, the peril they were in. The mercy Daniel and his fellows prayed for, was bestowed. The fervent prayers of righteous men avail much. Daniel was thankful to God for making known that to him, which saved the lives of himself and his fellows. How much more should we be thankful to God, for making known the great salvation of the soul to those who are not among the worldly wise and prudent!
2.m:
Daniel asked God to help him understand the king’s dream and what it meant. Having friends who pray for you is very helpful. Even important people should ask others to pray for them too. We should let our friends know we care by asking them to pray for us.

They were specific in their prayers. Whatever we ask for, we need to remember that only God can give it to us. In prayer, we can share our needs and troubles with God. 

Their main reason for praying was because they were in danger. When Daniel and his friends prayed, God answered their request. Prayer from good people really makes a difference. 

Daniel thanked God for telling him about the dream, which saved the lives of himself and his friends. Just imagine how grateful we should be if God tells us about the way to save our souls, even though we might not be smart or rich!

2.o: Daniel takes away the king's opinion of his magicians and soothsayers. The insufficiency of creatures should drive us to the all-sufficiency of the Creator. There is One who can do that for us, and make known that to us, which none on earth can, particularly the work of redemption, and the secret designs of God's love to us therein. Daniel confirmed the king in his opinion, that the dream was of great consequence, relating to the affairs and changes of this lower world. Let those whom God has highly favoured and honoured, lay aside all opinion of their own wisdom and worthiness, that the Lord alone may be praised for the good they have and do.
2.m:
Daniel shows the king that his magicians and fortune-tellers aren't helpful. When we see that other people can’t help us, we should turn to God instead. He’s the one who knows everything and can tell us things others don’t know, especially about God’s plan to save us.

Daniel helps the king believe that the dream is very important and relates to what will happen in the world. People who receive special blessings from God should remember that only God deserves credit for the good things they have and do.

2.o: This image represented the kingdoms of the earth, that should successively rule the nations, and influence the affairs of the Jewish church. 1. The head of gold signified the Chaldean empire, then in being. 2. The breast and arms of silver signified the empire of the Medes and Persians. 3. The belly and thighs of brass signified the Grecian empire, founded by Alexander. 4. The legs and feet of iron signified the Roman empire. The Roman empire branched into ten kingdoms, as the toes of these feet. Some were weak as clay, others strong as iron. Endeavours have often been used to unite them, for strengthening the empire, but in vain. The stone cut out without hands, represented the kingdom of our Lord Jesus Christ, which should be set up in the kingdoms of the world, upon the ruins of Satan's kingdom in them. This was the Stone which the builders refused, because it was not cut out by their hands, but it is become the head stone of the corner. Of the increase of Christ's government and peace there shall be no end. The Lord shall reign, not only to the end of time, but when time and days shall be no more. As far as events have gone, the fulfilling this prophetic vision has been most exact and undeniable; future ages shall witness this Stone destroying the image, and filling the whole earth.
2.m:
This image showed the different kingdoms that would rule over nations and affect the Jewish people. 

1. The head made of gold stood for the Babylonian Empire, which was around at that time.
2. The chest and arms made of silver stood for the empires of Media and Persia.
3. The belly and thighs made of bronze stood for the Greek Empire, started by Alexander the Great.
4. The legs and feet made of iron stood for the Roman Empire. It split into ten smaller kingdoms, shown as the toes on those feet. Some parts were weak like clay, while others were strong like iron. People tried many times to join these parts together to strengthen the empire, but failed.

The rock that wasn't cut by human hands stood for the kingdom of Jesus Christ. He would establish his kingdom among the nations after defeating Satan’s kingdom. This was the rejected stone that became the chief cornerstone. 

Jesus' kingdom will grow and bring lasting peace. God will rule forever, even beyond the end of time. So far, everything in this prophecy has come true very clearly. Future generations will see this stone destroy the image and fill the entire earth.

2.o: It is our business to direct attention to the Lord, as the Author and Giver of every good gift. Many have thoughts of the Divine power and majesty, who do not think of serving God themselves. But all should strive, that God may be glorified, and the best interests of mankind furthered.
2.m: Our job is to focus on the Lord, who gives us all good things. Many people think about God’s power but don’t try to serve him themselves. Everyone should work hard so that God gets glory and people everywhere benefit.

3.o: In the height of the image, about thirty yards, probably is included a pedestal, and most likely it was only covered with plates of gold, not a solid mass of that precious metal. Pride and bigotry cause men to require their subjects to follow their religion, whether right or wrong, and when worldly interest allures, and punishment overawes, few refuse. This is easy to the careless, the sensual, and the infidel, who are the greatest number; and most will go their ways. There is nothing so bad which the careless world will not be drawn to by a concert of music, or driven to by a fiery furnace. By such methods, false worship has been set up and maintained.
3.m:
At the top of the statue, about thirty yards high, there was probably a pedestal. The statue was likely covered with gold plates, not made entirely of solid gold.

Pride and stubbornness lead people to force others to follow their beliefs, even if those beliefs are wrong. When there’s an incentive for following along and punishment for disobeying, most people will obey. 

This is especially true for lazy, pleasure-seeking, and unbelieving people, who make up the majority. Most of them will just do what they’re told. 

There isn’t anything so bad that lazy people won’t join in if there’s music playing or be scared into doing it if there’s a fiery furnace waiting for them. False worship gets started and kept going using these kinds of tricks.

3.o: True devotion calms the spirit, quiets and softens it, but superstition and devotion to false gods inflame men's passions. The matter is put into a little compass, Turn, or burn. Proud men are still ready to say, as Nebuchadnezzar, Who is the Lord, that I should fear his power? Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego did not hesitate whether they should comply or not. Life or death were not to be considered. Those that would avoid sin, must not parley with temptation when that to which we are allured or affrighted is manifestly evil. Stand not to pause about it, but say, as Christ did, Get thee behind me, Satan. They did not contrive an evasive answer, when a direct answer was expected. Those who make their duty their main care, need not be anxious or fearful concerning the event. The faithful servants of God find him able to control and overrule all the powers armed against them. Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst. If He be for us, we need not fear what man can do unto us. God will deliver us, either from death or in death. They must obey God rather than man; they must rather suffer than sin; and must not do evil that good may come. Therefore none of these things moved them. The saving them from sinful compliance, was as great a miracle in the kingdom of grace, as the saving them out of the fiery furnace was in the kingdom of nature. Fear of man and love of the world, especially want of faith, make men yield to temptation, while a firm persuasion of the truth will deliver them from denying Christ, or being ashamed of him. We are to be meek in our replies, but we must be decided that we will obey God rather than man.
3.m:
True devotion helps calm people down, but superstition and worshiping false gods make people angry. It comes down to two choices: turn or burn. Arrogant people often ask, “Who is the Lord that I should fear?” But Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego didn’t hesitate between choosing to obey or not. For them, life or death wasn’t important. To avoid sin, you shouldn't think too long when tempted by something clearly wrong. Just say, “Get behind me, Satan,” like Jesus did. 

They didn’t try to dodge answering directly when asked. People focused on doing right don’t worry much about what happens next. Faithful followers of God see Him controlling even powerful enemies. So they trust, saying, “Lord, if you want to, you can.” When God is on our side, we don’t fear what humans might do. God will save us, either from danger or through it. 

These brave ones chose to follow God instead of people. They’d rather face trouble than break His rules. And they wouldn’t do bad things just because someone told them to. That’s why nothing could make them waver. Staying true to God was as big a miracle as getting saved from fire. 

Fear of others and love for worldly things, especially lack of faith, can lead people to give in to temptations. But believing firmly in the truth keeps us from denying or feeling shame about following Christ. We should be gentle in our answers, but strong in deciding to obey God more than anyone else.

3.o: Let Nebuchadnezzar heat his furnace as hot as he can, a few minutes will finish the torment of those cast into it; but hell-fire tortures, and yet does not kill. Those who worshipped the beast and his image, have no rest, no pause, no moment free from pain, Rev. 14:10,11. Now was fulfilled in the letter that great promise, Is. 43:2, When thou walkest through the fire, thou shalt not be burned. Leaving it to that God who preserved them in the fire, to bring them out, they walked up and down in the midst, supported and encouraged by the presence of the Son of God. Those who suffer for Christ, have his presence in their sufferings, even in the fiery furnace, and in the valley of the shadow of death. Nebuchadnezzar owns them for servants of the most high God; a God able to deliver them out of his hand. It is our God only is the consuming fire, Heb. 12:29. Could we but see into the eternal world, we should behold the persecuted believer safe from the malice of his foes, while they are exposed to the wrath of God, and tormented in unquenchable fires.
3.m:
Let Nebuchadnezzar heat his furnace as hot as he wants, but a few minutes will end the suffering of those thrown into it. But the flames of hell burn forever without killing anyone. People who worshiped the beast and its image feel no relief—no break from pain at all, Revelation 14:10-11 tells us. 

Now came true the promise in Isaiah 43:2: “When you walk through the fire, you won’t be burned.” They trusted God to keep them safe in the fire. The Son of God was there with them, supporting and encouraging them. 

Those who suffer for Christ have him with them, even in the worst places, like a fiery furnace or a dark valley. 

Nebuchadnezzar admits they are servants of the highest God, one who can save them from danger. Only our God is the burning fire, Hebrews 12:29 reminds us. 

If we could see the eternal world, we would find that believers who were persecuted are safe from their enemies, while those enemies face God’s anger and endless torture.

3.o: What God did for these his servants, would help to keep the Jews to their religion while in captivity, and to cure them of idolatry. The miracle brought deep convictions on Nebuchadnezzar. But no abiding change then took place in his conduct. He who preserved these pious Jews in the fiery furnace, is able to uphold us in the hour of temptation, and to keep us from falling into sin.
3.m:
What God did for these servants helped the Jews stay faithful to their religion even while they were captives. It also cured them of worshiping idols. The miracle made Nebuchadnezzar feel deeply guilty. But he didn't really change his ways. 

The same God who kept those good Jews safe in the fire can protect us too during times of trouble. He can help us avoid sinning.

4.o: The beginning and end of this chapter lead us to hope, that Nebuchadnezzar was a monument of the power of Divine grace, and of the riches of Divine mercy. After he was recovered from his madness, he told to distant places, and wrote down for future ages, how God had justly humbled and graciously restored him. When a sinner comes to himself, he will promote the welfare of others, by making known the wondrous mercy of God. Nebuchadnezzar, before he related the Divine judgments upon him for his pride, told the warnings he had in a dream or vision. The meaning was explained to him. The person signified, was to be put down from honour, and to be deprived of the use of his reason seven years. This is surely the sorest of all temporal judgments. Whatever outward affliction God is pleased to lay upon us, we have cause to bear it patiently, and to be thankful that he continues the use of our reason, and the peace of our consciences. Yet if the Lord should see fit by such means to keep a sinner from multiplying crimes, or a believer from dishonouring his name, even the dreadful prevention would be far preferable to the evil conduct. God has determined it, as a righteous Judge, and the angels in heaven applaud. Not that the great God needs the counsel or concurrence of the angels, but it denotes the solemnity of this sentence. The demand is by the word of the holy ones, God's suffering people: when the oppressed cry to God, he will hear. Let us diligently seek blessings which can never be taken from us, and especially beware of pride and forgetfulness of God.
4.m:
This chapter starts and ends with hope that Nebuchadnezzar became a symbol of God’s powerful grace and rich mercy. After getting better from his madness, he spread the news far and wide about how God had punished him fairly and then brought him back. When someone realizes their mistakes, they often help others by sharing stories of God’s amazing kindness.

Nebuchadnezzar talked about the dreams or visions he had before telling about God’s judgment on him because of his pride. In these dreams, he learned what would happen. A person would lose their honor and reasoning skills for seven years. Losing one’s mind for so long is surely the worst punishment anyone could face. 

Whatever troubles God sends us, we should accept them calmly and thank Him for keeping our minds clear and our consciences at peace. Even if God stops us from doing more wrong things or shaming His name, that severe action might actually be better than continuing in sin.

God decided this as a fair judge, and the angels praise Him. But God doesn’t need advice from angels; this shows how seriously He made this decision. The message came from God’s faithful people who were suffering. When those who are hurt pray to God, He listens.

We should look for blessings that can never be taken away, especially avoiding pride and forgetting God.

4.o: Daniel was struck with amazement and terror at so heavy a judgment coming upon so great a prince, and gives advice with tenderness and respect. It is necessary, in repentance, that we not only cease to do evil, but learn to do good. Though it might not wholly prevent the judgment, yet the trouble may be longer before it comes, or shorter when it does come. And everlasting misery will be escaped by all who repent and turn to God.
4.m:
Daniel was amazed and scared by the severe punishment coming to such a great ruler. He gave advice gently and respectfully. 

When we repent, we need to stop doing wrong things and start doing good ones. Even if it doesn’t completely stop the punishment, it might delay it or make it less harsh. 

Anyone who repents and turns back to God avoids eternal suffering.

4.o: Pride and self-conceit are sins that beset great men. They are apt to take that glory to themselves which is due to God only. While the proud word was in the king's mouth, the powerful word came from God. His understanding and his memory were gone, and all the powers of the rational soul were broken. How careful we ought to be, not to do any thing which may provoke God to put us out of our senses! God resists the proud. Nebuchadnezzar would be more than a man, but God justly makes him less than a man. We may learn to believe concerning God, that the most high God lives for ever, and that his kingdom is like himself, everlasting, and universal. His power cannot be resisted. When men are brought to honour God, by confession of sin and acknowledging his sovereignty, then, and not till then, they may expect that God will honour them; not only restore them to the dignity they lost by the sin of the first Adam, but add excellent majesty to them, from the righteousness and grace of the Second Adam. Afflictions shall last no longer than till they have done the work for which they were sent. There can be no reasonable doubt that Nebuchadnezzar was a true penitent, and an accepted believer. It is thought that he did not live more than a year after his restoration. Thus the Lord knows how to abase those that walk in pride, but gives grace and consolation to the humble, broken-hearted sinner who calls upon Him.
4.m:
Pride and self-importance often trouble important people. They think they deserve praise that really belongs to God alone. As soon as King Nebuchadnezzar started talking proudly, God spoke strongly against him. The king’s mind became confused, and he could no longer remember things clearly. All the abilities of his thinking part were ruined. 

We should be very careful not to do anything that might upset God and cause us to lose our minds. God opposes those who are arrogant. Nebuchadnezzar wanted to be greater than human, so God made him act less than human. This teaches us to trust that the Most High God lives forever and rules over everything. Nothing can stop his power.

When people honor God by admitting their wrongs and recognizing his authority, then God honors them too. He restores their former greatness and even adds extra glory through Jesus’ righteousness and grace. Troubles continue until they serve their purpose, then they end.

Many think Nebuchadnezzar truly repented and believed. Some say he lived only one year after being restored. God knows how to bring down those who are proud, but shows mercy to humble, repentant sinners who call on him.

5.o: Belshazzar bade defiance to the judgments of God. Most historians consider that Cyrus then besieged Babylon. Security and sensuality are sad proofs of approaching ruin. That mirth is sinful indeed, which profanes sacred things; and what are many of the songs used at modern feasts better than the praises sung by the heathens to their gods! See how God struck terror upon Belshazzar and his lords. God's written word is enough to put the proudest, boldest sinner in a fright. What we see of God, the part of the hand that writes in the book of the creatures, and in the book of the Scriptures, should fill us with awful thoughts concerning that part which we do not see. If this be the finger of God, what is his arm when made bare? And what is He? The king's guilty conscience told him that he had no reason to expect any good news from heaven. God can, in a moment, make the heart of the stoutest sinner to tremble; and there needs no more than to let loose his own thoughts upon him; they will give him trouble enough. No bodily pain can equal the inward agony which sometimes seizes the sinner in the midst of mirth, carnal pleasures, and worldly pomp. Sometimes terrors cause a man to flee to Christ for pardon and peace; but many cry out for fear of wrath, who are not humbled for their sins, and who seek relief by lying vanities. The ignorance and uncertainty concerning the Holy Scriptures, shown by many who call themselves wise, only tend to drive sinners to despair, as the ignorance of these wise men did.
5.m:
Belshazzar ignored God’s punishments. Many historians think that Cyrus was attacking Babylon at that time. Feeling safe and enjoying life too much are signs of coming disaster. Mirth becomes sinful when it dishonors holy things. Are the songs people sing at parties today any better than the praises pagans sang to their gods?

Notice how God scared Belshazzar and his officials. Just seeing God’s words in nature and in the Bible can terrify even the most proud and bold sinners. When we see God’s hand writing in creation and in scripture, it makes us wonder about all the parts we don’t see. If this is just the finger of God, imagine His full strength!

The king felt guilty and knew he shouldn't expect good news from God. God can scare the bravest sinner instantly. All it takes is letting our thoughts run wild, and they’ll bring plenty of worry. Physical pain cannot compare to the inner torment some sinners feel during joyous times filled with pleasure and wealth.

Sometimes fear drives people to Jesus for forgiveness and peace. But others scream out of fear without truly repenting and seeking true comfort. Ignorance about the Holy Scriptures among those claiming wisdom often leads sinners to despair, just as these wise men’s lack of knowledge did.

5.o: Daniel was forgotten at court; he lived privately, and was then ninety years of age. Many consult servants of God on curious questions, or to explain difficult subjects, but without asking the way of salvation, or the path of duty. Daniel slighted the offer of reward. He spoke to Belshazzar as to a condemned criminal. We should despise all the gifts and rewards this world can give, did we see, as we may by faith, its end hastening on; but let us do our duty in the world, and do it all the real service we can.
5.m:
Daniel was forgotten at court. He lived quietly and was then ninety years old. 

Many people ask God’s servants about strange things or hard topics, but they don’t ask about how to be saved or what is right to do.

Daniel didn’t want the reward offered. 

He talked to Belshazzar as if he were a guilty prisoner.

We should ignore all the gifts and rewards this world offers, especially when we see how quickly it will end. But we should still do our duties well and help others as much as we can.

5.o: Daniel reads Belshazzar's doom. He had not taken warning by the judgments upon Nebuchadnezzar. And he had insulted God. Sinners are pleased with gods that neither see, nor hear, nor know; but they will be judged by One to whom all things are open. Daniel reads the sentence written on the wall. All this may well be applied to the doom of every sinner. At death, the sinner's days are numbered and finished; after death is the judgment, when he will be weighed in the balance, and found wanting; and after judgment the sinner will be cut asunder, and given as a prey to the devil and his angels. While these things were passing in the palace, it is considered that the army of Cyrus entered the city; and when Belshazzar was slain, a general submission followed. Soon will every impenitent sinner find the writing of God's word brought to pass upon him, whether he is weighed in the balance of the law as a self-righteous Pharisee, or in that of the gospel as a painted hypocrite.
5.m:
Daniel tells Belshazzar what will happen to him. Belshazzar did not learn from the punishments that came to Nebuchadnezzar before him. He also insulted God. People who sin like to worship gods that cannot see, hear, or know anything. But one day, they will face someone who knows everything about them.

Daniel reads the message written on the wall. This shows us what happens to everyone who sins. When we die, our time here ends. After that comes judgment, where we are measured against God’s standards and found lacking. Then, those who do not turn away from their sins are punished and handed over to evil spirits.

While all this was happening inside the palace, an army led by Cyrus entered the city. When Belshazzar died, everyone surrendered. 

Soon, anyone who does not repent will see God’s words come true for them. Whether they try to follow the law perfectly or pretend to be good, they will still be judged.

6.o: We notice to the glory of God, that though Daniel was now very old, yet he was able for business, and had continued faithful to his religion. It is for the glory of God, when those who profess religion, conduct themselves so that their most watchful enemies may find no occasion for blaming them, save only in the matters of their God, in which they walk according to their consciences.
6.m: We see to God’s honor that even at an old age, Daniel stayed strong and remained true to his faith. This shows God’s glory because people who claim to follow religion behave well enough that their opponents cannot find fault with them except in matters related to their God, where they act according to their beliefs.

6.o: To forbid prayer for thirty days, is, for so long, to rob God of all the tribute he has from man, and to rob man of all the comfort he has in God. Does not every man's heart direct him, when in want or distress, to call upon God? We could not live a day without God; and can men live thirty days without prayer? Yet it is to be feared that those who, without any decree forbidding them, present no hearty, serious petitions to God for more than thirty days together, are far more numerous than those who serve him continually, with humble, thankful hearts. Persecuting laws are always made on false pretences; but it does not become Christians to make bitter complaints, or to indulge in revilings. It is good to have hours for prayer. Daniel prayed openly and avowedly; and though a man of vast business, he did not think that would excuse him from daily exercises of devotion. How inexcusable are those who have but little to do in the world, yet will not do thus much for their souls! In trying times we must take heed, lest, under pretence of discretion, we are guilty of cowardice in the cause of God. All who throw away their souls, as those certainly do that live without prayer, even if it be to save their lives, at the end will be found to be fools. Nor did Daniel only pray, and not give thanks, cutting off some part of the service to make the time of danger shorter; but he performed the whole. In a word, the duty of prayer is founded upon the sufficiency of God as an almighty Creator and Redeemer, and upon our wants as sinful creatures. To Christ we must turn our eyes. Thither let the Christian look, thither let him pray, in this land of his captivity.
6.m:
Forbidding people from praying for thirty days means taking away all the help and comfort they get from God during that time. When someone is in need or trouble, don’t they naturally turn to God? Can anyone survive a single day without God’s help? And yet, can they really go thirty days without praying?

It’s sad that many people choose not to pray regularly, even when there’s nothing stopping them. They might not pray seriously for weeks, while others keep praying humbly and thankfully every day.

People who try to stop others from praying usually lie about their reasons. But Christians shouldn’t complain bitterly or use harsh words. 

Having set times for prayer is helpful. Daniel prayed openly and honestly, even though he was very busy. He didn’t think being busy excused him from praying each day. Those who have less to do should still find time to pray for their souls.

In tough times, we must be careful not to hide behind excuses of caution and end up being cowards for God’s sake. People who abandon prayer to save their lives show foolishness in the end.

Daniel didn’t just stop praying; he also stopped giving thanks. Instead, he kept doing everything he was supposed to do. Prayer is based on God’s power as creator and savior, and on our needs as sinners.

Christians should focus their prayers on Jesus. That’s where they should look and pray, even when they’re in difficult situations.

6.o: It is no new thing for what is done faithfully, in conscience toward God, to be misrepresented as done obstinately, and in contempt of the civil powers. Through want of due thought, we often do that which afterwards, like Darius, we see cause a thousand times to wish undone again. Daniel, that venerable man, is brought as the vilest of malefactors, and is thrown into the den of lions, to be devoured, only for worshipping his God. No doubt the placing the stone was ordered by the providence of God, that the miracle of Daniel's deliverance might appear more plain; and the king sealed it with his own signet, probably lest Daniel's enemies should kill him. Let us commit our lives and souls unto God, in well-doing. We cannot place full confidence even in men whom we faithfully serve; but believers may, in all cases, be sure of the Divine favour and consolation.
6.m:
It’s not unusual for people who act faithfully towards God to be wrongly seen as stubbornly defying human authorities. Often, without thinking carefully, we do things later we regret, just like King Darius did many times. The good man Daniel was treated like a criminal and put in a lion’s den simply because he worshipped his God. 

Placing the stone over the entrance was likely part of God’s plan so that Daniel’s rescue would be obvious. The king himself sealed the stone with his ring, possibly to stop Daniel’s enemies from harming him. 

We should trust God with our lives and actions when we do what is right. Even those we serve loyally might let us down, but true believers can always count on God’s help and comfort.

6.o: The best way to have a good night, is to keep a good conscience. We are sure of what the king doubted, that the servants of the living God have a Master well able to protect them. See the power of God over the fiercest creatures, and believe his power to restrain the roaring lion that goeth about continually seeking to devour. Daniel was kept perfectly safe, because he believed in his God. Those who boldly and cheerfully trust in God to protect them in the way of duty, shall always find him a present help. Thus the righteous is delivered out of trouble, and the wicked cometh in his stead. The short triumph of the wicked will end in their ruin.
6.m:
Having a good night means keeping a clear conscience. We know for certain what the king wasn’t sure about: the servants of the living God have a Master who can protect them. 

See how powerful God is over even the fiercest animals. Believe that he has the strength to stop the fierce lion that roams around looking to hurt people. 

Daniel stayed completely safe because he trusted in his God. People who bravely and happily rely on God to keep them safe while doing what’s right will always find him there helping them. 

So, the righteous person gets rescued from danger, and the wicked one takes their place. The brief victory of the wicked ends in their destruction.

6.o: If we live in the fear of God, and walk according to that rule, peace shall be upon us. The kingdom, the power, and the glory, for ever, are the Lord's; but many are employed in making known his wonderful works to others, who themselves remain strangers to his saving grace. May we be doers, as well as believers of his word, least at the last we should be found to have deceived ourselves.
6.m: If we live in fear of God and follow his rules, peace will be with us. The kingdom, power, and glory belong to the Lord forever. But many people tell others about his amazing works without knowing his saving grace themselves. Let us be people who both believe and obey his word, so we don't fool ourselves in the end.

7.o: This vision contains the same prophetic representations with Nebuchadnezzar's dream. The great sea agitated by the winds, represented the earth and the dwellers on it troubled by ambitious princes and conquerors. The four beasts signified the same four empires, as the four parts of Nebuchadnezzar's image. Mighty conquerors are but instruments of God's vengeance on a guilty world. The savage beast represents the hateful features of their characters. But the dominion given to each has a limit; their wrath shall be made to praise the Lord, and the remainder of it he will restrain.
7.m:
This vision shows the same things as Nebuchadnezzar's dream. 

The stormy sea stood for the earth and its people being upset by powerful rulers and conquerors. 

The four animals symbolized the same four empires shown in the different parts of Nebuchadnezzar’s statue. 

Great conquerors are just tools God uses to punish a sinful world. 

These wild beasts showed the terrible qualities of these leaders. 

But there was an end to the power given to each one. Their anger would eventually honor God, and He would control what was left of it.

7.o: These verses are for the comfort and support of the people of God, in reference to the persecutions that would come upon them. Many New Testament predictions of the judgment to come, have plain allusion to this vision; especially Rev. 20:11,12. The Messiah is here called the Son of man; he was made in the likeness of sinful flesh, and was found in fashion as a man, but he is the Son of God. The great event foretold in this passage, is Christ's glorious coming, to destroy every antichristian power, and to render his own kingdom universal upon earth. But ere the solemn time arrives, for manifesting the glory of God to all worlds in his dealings with his creatures, we may expect that the doom of each of us will be determined at the hour of our death; and before the end shall come, the Father will openly give to his incarnate Son, our Mediator and Judge, the inheritance of the nations as his willing subjects.
7.m:
These verses bring comfort and support to God’s people during times of persecution. Many predictions in the New Testament about future judgment relate to this vision, especially Revelation 20:11,12. Here, the Messiah is called the Son of Man. He took on human form, just like us, yet he is the Son of God. 

The main event described is Jesus’ return to defeat all powers against him and spread his kingdom worldwide. Before the final day when God shows his glory to everyone, each person’s fate will be decided at their death. And before everything ends, the Father will publicly give Jesus, our Mediator and Judge, rule over all nations who willingly follow him.

7.o: It is desirable to obtain the right and full sense of what we see and hear from God; and those that would know, must ask by faithful and fervent prayer. The angel told Daniel plainly. He especially desired to know respecting the little horn, which made war with the saints, and prevailed against them. Here is foretold the rage of papal Rome against true Christians. St. John, in his visions and prophecies, which point in the first place at Rome, has plain reference to these visions. Daniel had a joyful prospect of the prevalence of God's kingdom among men. This refers to the second coming of our blessed Lord, when the saints shall triumph in the complete fall of Satan's kingdom. The saints of the Most High shall possess the kingdom for ever. Far be it from us to infer from hence, that dominion is founded on grace. It promises that the gospel kingdom shall be set up; a kingdom of light, holiness, and love; a kingdom of grace, the privileges and comforts of which shall be the earnest and first-fruits of the kingdom of glory. But the full accomplishment will be in the everlasting happiness of the saints, the kingdom that cannot be moved. The gathering together the whole family of God will be a blessedness of Christ's coming.
7.m:
It’s important to fully understand what we see and hear from God. To learn more, we need to pray faithfully and sincerely. 

The angel explained things clearly to Daniel. Daniel wanted to know about the little horn that fought against the saints and won. This prophecy talks about the anger of the Roman Catholic Church toward real Christians.

St. John saw similar visions in his prophecies, pointing mainly to Rome.

Daniel was given good news about God’s kingdom growing stronger among people. This points to Jesus’ return, when the saints will celebrate the total defeat of Satan’s rule.

God’s holy people will have the kingdom forever. We shouldn’t think that power comes only through kindness. Instead, it means that God’s kingdom of truth, goodness, and love will grow. People will enjoy its blessings before the final heavenly kingdom arrives.

In the end, all of God’s people will join together in joy when Jesus returns, bringing eternal happiness.

8.o: God gives Daniel a foresight of the destruction of other kingdoms, which in their day were as powerful as that of Babylon. Could we foresee the changes that shall be when we are gone, we should be less affected with changes in our own day. The ram with two horns was the second empire, that of Media and Persia. He saw this ram overcome by a he-goat. This was Alexander the Great. Alexander, when about thirty-three years of age, and in his full strength, died, and showed the vanity of worldly pomp and power, and that they cannot make a man happy. While men dispute, as in the case of Alexander, respecting the death of some prosperous warrior, it is plain that the great First Cause of all had no more of his plan for him to execute, and therefore cut him off. Instead of that one great horn, there came up four notable ones, Alexander's four chief captains. A little horn became a great persecutor of the church and people of God. It seems that the Mohammedan delusion is here pointed out. It prospered, and at one time nearly destroyed the holy religion God's right hand had planted. It is just with God to deprive those of the privileges of his house who despise and profane them; and to make those know the worth of ordinances by the want of them, who would not know it by the enjoyment of them. Daniel heard the time of this calamity limited and determined; but not the time when it should come. If we would know the mind of God, we must apply to Christ, in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge; not hid from us, but hid for us. There is much difficulty as to the precise time here stated, but the end of it cannot be very distant. God will, for his own glory, see to the cleansing of the church in due time. Christ died to cleanse his church; and he will so cleanse it as to present it blameless to himself.
8.m:
God shows Daniel what will happen to other strong kingdoms after Babylon falls. If we could see future changes, we wouldn't worry as much about today’s changes.

Daniel sees a vision of a ram with two horns. These represent the Medes and Persians. Then he sees a goat defeat the ram. The goat is Alexander the Great. When Alexander was around 33 years old and at his strongest, he died. This shows that earthly power doesn’t bring true happiness.

Some people wonder if Alexander’s death was an accident. But really, God decided it was time for him to die because his part in God’s plan was done.

After Alexander died, four smaller powers took over. One of these became a big enemy of God’s people. This might refer to the Muslim rulers who tried to destroy Christianity.

This enemy grew stronger and almost wiped out God’s true faith. God takes away blessings from those who don’t respect them. People learn the value of God’s teachings only when they lose them.

Daniel learns when this trouble will start, but not exactly when it ends. To truly understand God’s plans, we need to turn to Jesus, who holds all wisdom and truth.

There are many details about timing that aren’t clear. But the end is near. God will clean His church and show His glory.

Jesus died to purify His church. And He will do it perfectly, presenting the church without fault to Himself.

8.o: The eternal Son of God stood before the prophet in the appearance of a man, and directed the angel Gabriel to explain the vision. Daniel's fainting and astonishment at the prospect of evils he saw coming on his people and the church, confirm the opinion that long-continued calamities were foretold. The vision being ended, a charge was given to Daniel to keep it private for the present. He kept it to himself, and went on to do the duty of his place. As long as we live in this world we must have something to do in it; and even those whom God has most honoured, must not think themselves above their business. Nor must the pleasure of communion with God take us from the duties of our callings, but we must in them abide with God. All who are intrusted with public business must discharge their trust uprightly; and, amidst all doubts and discouragements, they may, if true believers, look forward to a happy issue. Thus should we endeavour to compose our minds for attending to the duties to which each is appointed, in the church and in the world.
8.m:
The eternal Son of God appeared to the prophet as a human and told the angel Gabriel to explain the vision. Daniel felt weak and shocked by the troubles he foresaw for his people and the church. This confirms that the vision showed ongoing disasters ahead.

After the vision ended, Daniel was told to keep it secret for now. He did so and continued doing his work. While we live in this world, we always have tasks to complete. Even those honored by God still need to fulfill their responsibilities. We shouldn’t let time spent with God distract us from our jobs. Instead, we should stay focused while working.

People entrusted with important tasks must be honest about them. Even when facing doubts and fears, true believers can hope for a good outcome. We should try to calm ourselves and focus on the duties assigned to us, whether in the church or the world.

9.o: Daniel learned from the books of the prophets, especially from Jeremiah, that the desolation of Jerusalem would continue seventy years, which were drawing to a close. God's promises are to encourage our prayers, not to make them needless; and when we see the performance of them approaching, we should more earnestly plead them with God.
9.m:
Daniel learned from the books of the prophets, especially Jeremiah, that the city of Jerusalem would be destroyed for seventy years. Those seventy years were almost over.

God’s promises are meant to give us hope and encourage us to pray, not to replace our need to ask for things. As these promises come closer to happening, we should keep asking God about them.

9.o: In every prayer we must make confession, not only of the sins we have been guilty of, but of our faith in God, and dependence upon him, our sorrow for sin, and our resolutions against it. It must be our confession, the language of our convictions. Here is Daniel's humble, serious, devout address to God; in which he gives glory to him as a God to be feared, and as a God to be trusted. We should, in prayer, look both at God's greatness and his goodness, his majesty and mercy. Here is a penitent confession of sin, the cause of the troubles the people for so many years groaned under. All who would find mercy must thus confess their sins. Here is a self-abasing acknowledgment of the righteousness of God; and it is evermore the way of true penitents thus to justify God. Afflictions are sent to bring men to turn from their sins, and to understand God's truth. Here is a believing appeal to the mercy of God. It is a comfort that God has been always ready to pardon sin. It is encouraging to recollect that mercies belong to God, as it is convincing and humbling to recollect that righteousness belongs to him. There are abundant mercies in God, not only forgiveness, but forgivenesses. Here are pleaded the reproach God's people was under, and the ruins God's sanctuary was in. Sin is a reproach to any people, especially to God's people. The desolations of the sanctuary are grief to all the saints. Here is an earnest request to God to restore the poor captive Jews to their former enjoyments. O Lord, hearken and do. Not hearken and speak only, but hearken and do; do that for us which none else can do; and defer not. Here are several pleas and arguments to enforce the petitions. Do it for the Lord Christ's sake; Christ is the Lord of all. And for his sake God causes his face to shine upon sinners when they repent, and turn to him. In all our prayers this must be our plea, we must make mention of his righteousness, even of his only. The humble, fervent, believing earnestness of this prayer should ever be followed by us.
9.m:
In every prayer, we need to admit our sins and show our trust in God. We should tell Him about our faith, sadness over our wrongs, and plans to avoid them. This is what Daniel did in his prayer. He spoke humbly and respectfully to God, praising Him as someone to fear and trust. 

We should remember both God’s power and His kindness in our prayers. Daniel admitted his sins and said sorry for them. Many problems came because of those sins. To get help from God, we must admit our mistakes too. 

Daniel also said that God is right to punish us. True believers always agree that God is just. Troubles teach us to stop doing bad things and learn God’s ways. 

Daniel asked God to forgive us, remembering that God has always been willing to pardon sins. Thinking about God’s mercy brings hope. But knowing His justice makes us humble. 

He talked about how God’s people were being shamed and how the temple was destroyed. Sins hurt God’s people more than others. Ruining the holy place hurts everyone who loves God. 

Then Daniel begged God to let the Jewish captives go home. “Lord, listen and act,” he said. Don’t just hear us, actually help us. Only You can fix these problems. 

He gave reasons for God to answer his prayer. For Jesus’ sake, please help us. When we turn back to God, He smiles on us through Jesus. Always ask God to honor Jesus in our prayers. 

The strong, sincere, hopeful way Daniel prayed shows us how to pray.

9.o: An answer was immediately sent to Daniel's prayer, and it is a very memorable one. We cannot now expect that God should send answers to our prayers by angels, but if we pray with fervency for that which God has promised, we may by faith take the promise as an immediate answer to the prayer; for He is faithful that has promised. Daniel had a far greater and more glorious redemption discovered to him, which God would work out for his church in the latter days. Those who would be acquainted with Christ and his grace, must be much in prayer. The evening offering was a type of the great sacrifice Christ was to offer in the evening of the world: in virtue of that sacrifice Daniel's prayer was accepted; and for the sake of that, this glorious discovery of redeeming love was made to him. We have, in verses 24-27, one of the most remarkable prophecies of Christ, of his coming and his salvation. It shows that the Jews are guilty of most obstinate unbelief, in expecting another Messiah, so long after the time expressly fixed for his coming. The seventy weeks mean a day for a year, or 490 years. About the end of this period a sacrifice would be offered, making full atonement for sin, and bringing in everlasting righteousness for the complete justification of every believer. Then the Jews, in the crucifixion of Jesus, would commit that crime by which the measure of their guilt would be filled up, and troubles would come upon their nation. All blessings bestowed on sinful man come through Christ's atoning sacrifice, who suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, that he might bring us to God. Here is our way of access to the throne of grace, and of our entrance to heaven. This seals the sum of prophecy, and confirms the covenant with many; and while we rejoice in the blessings of salvation, we should remember what they cost the Redeemer. How can those escape who neglect so great salvation!
9.m:
Daniel’s prayer was quickly answered, and it was a very important answer. Today, God doesn’t usually send answers to our prayers through angels, but if we pray strongly about something God has promised, we can see His promise as an immediate answer to our prayer because He keeps His promises.

Daniel learned about a future rescue that God would do for His people. To know Christ and His grace, we need to spend lots of time praying. 

The evening sacrifice was a symbol of the big sacrifice Christ would make later. Because of this future sacrifice, Daniel’s prayer was accepted. And because of it, Daniel got to learn about God’s plan to save people.

In verses 24-27, there is a famous prophecy about Christ’s coming and His saving work. It shows that the Jewish people were being stubbornly unfaithful by waiting for someone else to be the Messiah, even though the exact time for His arrival was clearly stated.

The seventy weeks mentioned here means 490 years. At the end of this time, a perfect sacrifice would be made to cover all sins and bring eternal righteousness to believers. But during this time, the Jews would kill Jesus, completing their guilt and leading to great trouble for them.

All the good things given to sinful people come from Christ’s sacrifice, where He died once for everyone’s sins. Through Him, we get forgiveness and go to heaven.

This prophecy tells us how to approach God and enter heaven. It confirms God’s agreement with many people. While we celebrate the blessings of salvation, let’s remember what Christ went through to give us these gifts. How can anyone ignore such a great gift?

10.o: Whenever we enter into communion with God, it becomes us to have a due sense of the infinite distance between us and the holy God. How shall we, that are dust and ashes, speak to the Lord of glory? Nothing is more likely, nothing more effectual to revive the drooping spirits of the saints, than to be assured of God's love to them. From the very first day we begin to look toward God in a way of duty, he is ready to meet us in the way of mercy. Thus ready is God to hear prayer. When the angel had told the prophet of the things to come, he was to return, and oppose the decrees of the Persian kings against the Jews. The angels are employed as God's ministering servants, Heb. 1:14. Though much was done against the Jews by the kings of Persia, God permitting it, much more mischief would have been done if God had not prevented it. He would now more fully show what were God's purposes, of which the prophecies form an outline; and we are concerned to study what is written in these Scriptures of truth, for they belong to our everlasting peace. While Satan and his angels, and evil counsellors, excite princes to mischief against the church, we may rejoice that Christ our Prince, and all his mighty angels, act against our enemies; but we ought not to expect many to favour us in this evil world. Yet the whole counsel of God shall be established; and let each one pray, Lord Jesus, be our righteousness now, and thou wilt be our everlasting confidence, through life, in death, at the day of judgment, and for evermore.
10.m:
When we talk to God, we should remember how far apart we are from him. We are just dust and ashes compared to the glorious God. Knowing that God loves us helps lift our spirits. As soon as we start doing our duties towards God, he is ready to show mercy to us. This shows how eager God is to listen to our prayers.

After the angel told the prophet about future events, he was supposed to go back and fight against the plans of the Persian kings who were mean to the Jews. Angels work as God’s helpers, serving Him. Even though bad things happened because of those kings, worse things could have happened if God hadn’t stopped them.

God wanted to reveal His full plan, which the prophecies only hint at. It’s important for us to read the Scriptures carefully because they help us find peace forever.

While Satan and his followers try to cause trouble for the church, we can be happy knowing that Jesus and His powerful angels protect us. But don't expect too many people in this sinful world to support us. Still, everything God has planned will happen. So we should pray, “Lord Jesus, be our righteousness now, and you will be our eternal hope, through life, death, and on Judgment Day, forever.”

11.o: The angel shows Daniel the succession of the Persian and Grecian empires. The kings of Egypt and Syria are noticed: Judea was between their dominions, and affected by their contests. From Dan. 11:5-30, is generally considered to relate to the events which came to pass during the continuance of these governments; and from Dan. 11:21, to relate to Antiochus Epiphanes, who was a cruel and violent persecutor of the Jews. See what decaying, perishing things worldly pomp and possessions are, and the power by which they are gotten. God, in his providence, sets up one, and pulls down another, as he pleases. This world is full of wars and fightings, which come from men's lusts. All changes and revolutions of states and kingdoms, and every event, are plainly and perfectly foreseen by God. No word of God shall fall to the ground; but what he has designed, what he has declared, shall infallibly come to pass. While the potsherds of the earth strive with each other, they prevail and are prevailed against, deceive and are deceived; but those who know God will trust in him, and he will enable them to stand their ground, bear their cross, and maintain their conflict.
11.m:
The angel shows Daniel about the rise and fall of the Persian and Greek empires. He mentions the kings of Egypt and Syria because Judea was caught in the middle of their battles. 

From Daniel 11:5 to 30, most people think it talks about events that happened while these empires were ruling. And from Daniel 11:21, it focuses on Antiochus Epiphanes, who was very mean to the Jewish people.

Think about how short-lived and unimportant earthly wealth and power really are. God decides who gets power and who loses it. Our world is filled with wars and fights caused by human desires.

All changes in countries and kingdoms, and everything else that happens, are known by God. Not a single word from God fails. What he plans and declares will surely happen.

While people struggle against each other, some win and others lose, trick and get tricked. But those who know God can trust him. He helps them stay strong, carry their burdens, and face their challenges.

11.o: The remainder of this prophecy is very difficult, and commentators differ much respecting it. From Antiochus the account seems to pass to antichrist. Reference seems to be made to the Roman empire, the fourth monarchy, in its pagan, early Christian, and papal states. The end of the Lord's anger against his people approaches, as well as the end of his patience towards his enemies. If we would escape the ruin of the infidel, the idolater, the superstitious and cruel persecutor, as well as that of the profane, let us make the oracles of God our standard of truth and of duty, the foundation of our hope, and the light of our paths through this dark world, to the glorious inheritance above.
11.m:
This part of the prophecy is hard to understand, and experts disagree about what it means. It seems to go from talking about Antiochus to talking about someone who will oppose Christ at the end times. 

It might refer to the Roman Empire during different periods—when it was ruled by pagans, then early Christians, and finally by those who claim special power over religion. 

God’s anger toward his people is ending, and his patience with those who harm them is coming to an end too. To avoid the fate of unbelievers, those who worship idols, superstitious and cruel rulers, and those who misuse religion, we should follow God’s word for truth and guidance. Let it shape our beliefs and actions, give us hope, and help us see clearly on our journey through life to the wonderful reward waiting for us in heaven.

12.o: One of the angels asking how long it should be to the end of these wonders, a solemn reply is made, that it would be for a time, times, and a half, the period mentioned Dan. 7:25, and in the Revelation. It signifies 1260 prophetic days or years, beginning from the time when the power of the holy people should be scattered. The imposture of Mohammed, and the papal usurpation, began about the same time; and these were a twofold attack upon the church of God. But all will end well at last. All opposing rule, principality, and power, shall be put down, and holiness and love will triumph, and be in honour, to eternity. The end, this end, shall come. What an amazing prophecy is this, of so many varied events, and extending through so many successive ages, even to the general resurrection! Daniel must comfort himself with the pleasing prospect of his own happiness in death, in judgment, and to eternity. It is good for us all to think much of going away from this world. That must be our way; but it is our comfort that we shall not go till God calls us to another world, and till he has done with us in this world; till he says, Go thou thy way, thou hast done thy work, therefore now, go thy way, and leave it to others to take thy place. It was a comfort to Daniel, and is a comfort to all the saints, that whatever their lot is in the days of their lives, they shall have a happy lot in the end of the days. And it ought to be the great care and concern of every one of us to secure this. Then we may well be content with our present lot, and welcome the will of God. Believers are happy at all times; they rest in God by faith now, and a rest is reserved for them in heaven at last.
12.m:
An angel asked how long until the end of these troubles. A serious answer came back: it would be for a time, two times, and half a time—1260 prophetic days or years. This started when the power of God’s people was broken. 

The false teachings of Muhammad and the rise of the Pope both began around the same time. These were double attacks on God’s church. But everything will turn out fine in the end. All opposition will be defeated, and goodness and love will win forever. The end will come.

This is such a detailed prophecy, covering many different events over many years, even to the final resurrection. Daniel could find comfort knowing what would happen after he died—in judgment and forever. We should all think often about leaving this world. It’s good because God will call us to another world only when He’s finished with us here. When He says, “Go,” then we know our work is done.

Daniel found peace knowing that no matter what happens during his life, he’ll have a happy ending. This should give hope to everyone who believes. Whatever their situation now, believers will have joy later. So we can accept whatever comes our way and trust God’s plan. Believers are always happy because they rely on God now and look forward to eternal rest in heaven.
